frontmatter.title: Notes on models of type theory
Representable maps in terms of adjunctions

mainmatter.p: A morphism { E } \xrightarrow {{ p }}{ U } be a morphism in \operatorname {Pr} \mathcal {C} is representable if and only if the induced functor { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } } between categories of elements has a right adjoint.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Let there be a right adjoint { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } } to { \widetilde { E } } \xrightarrow {{ \widetilde { p } }}{ \widetilde { U } }, and fix a natural transformation { \Gamma } \xrightarrow {{ \chi }}{ U } with representable domain, i.e. an element \chi \in \widetilde { U }. We must check that the fiber product E \times _{U} \Gamma is representable.

Applying the right adjoint { \widetilde { U } } \xrightarrow {{ q }}{ \widetilde { E } }, we have an element q \chi \in \widetilde { E }, i.e. a morphism { \partial _0 { \left ( q \chi \right )} } \xrightarrow {{ q \chi }}{ E } whose (representable) domain we expect shall form the desired fiber product, but we must exhibit an appropriate projection { \partial _0 { \left ( q \chi \right )} } \xrightarrow {{ p_ \chi }}{ \Gamma } making the following square cartesian:

We consider the component of the counit { \widetilde { p } \circ q } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon }}{ 1 _{ \widetilde { U } } } at \chi \in \widetilde { U }, from which we obtain a morphism { \widetilde { p } { \left ( q \chi \right )} } \xrightarrow {{ \epsilon _ \chi }}{ \chi } in \widetilde { U }. Unraveling definitions, this is precisely a map making the square above commute, so we may define p_ \chi : \equiv \epsilon _ \chi. It suffices now to prove that the following square is cartesian:
Because pullbacks in presheaf categories are computed pointwise, it suffices to consider only squares with a representable apex, such as the one below in which \Delta is assumed representable:

To construct the universal map { \Delta } \xrightarrow {{ { \left \langle e, \gamma \right \rangle } }}{ \partial _0q \chi } factoring e, \gamma through q \chi , \epsilon _ \chi respectively, we note the square above corresponds preicsely to a morphism { \widetilde { p } e } \xrightarrow {{ \gamma }}{ \chi } in \widetilde { U }. We may visualise this as follows;
